operations.rationale: Replace the permission-class table with reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research taxonomy.
content_lines: The authority record has to name the kind of permission being requested, because the same information can be safe in one permission class and unsafe in another. Reading a source, transforming it into a summary, disclosing it to an audience, writing it into a durable artifact, executing a tool, and approving a high-impact action are different powers. A system that collapses them into a generic access flag has already lost the boundary it needs to enforce.

Read permission lets a layer inspect material without publishing it, mutating it, or acting on it. Transform permission lets a layer derive a new artifact, but it carries provenance and taint obligations. Disclosure permission asks whether the output may be shown, exported, or transmitted to the intended audience. Write permission changes a file, ledger, memory cell, branch, or other durable target and therefore needs target-scoped authority plus audit. Execute permission crosses into a tool, runtime, service, or adapter and should leave an effect receipt. Approval permission is still more constrained: it authorizes a promotion or high-impact action and cannot be self-issued by the actor that benefits from it.

These classes are deliberately ordinary. Most failures at this layer are ordinary too: a model reads something and treats the text as approval, a planner turns a draft into an action, a tool wrapper lets a low-authority request borrow a broader credential, or a replacement implementation inherits a handle that was qualified for an earlier context. The point of the taxonomy is to make those mistakes visible before they become side effects.
